concepts.name: Algebra for Logic
concepts.description: Boolean and Heyting algebras and abstract algebraic logic; the algebraic counterparts of classical and intuitionistic logic (Smith §12.1).

concepts.name: Boolean Algebras
concepts.description: The algebra of classical logic: lattices, Boolean operations, and the structures behind truth-functional semantics — also the engine of Boolean-valued models in set-theoretic independence arguments (Smith §12.1).

concepts.name: Heyting Algebras
concepts.description: Pseudo-Boolean algebras: the algebraic counterpart of intuitionistic logic, connected to Kripke's relational semantics (Smith §12.1).

concepts.name: Abstract Algebraic Logic
concepts.description: When does a propositional logic have a well-behaved algebraic counterpart? The general theory behind the classical–Boolean and intuitionistic–Heyting pairings (Smith §12.1).

concepts.name: Model Theory
concepts.description: The study of structures satisfying first-order theories: compactness, ultraproducts, and stability (Smith §12.2).

concepts.name: Ultraproducts
concepts.description: The ultraproduct construction: building new models from families of structures, yielding compactness and transfer arguments (Smith §12.2).

concepts.name: Stability Theory
concepts.description: Classification-theoretic model theory: stable theories and the modern program descending from Morley and Shelah (Smith §12.2).

concepts.name: Set Theory
concepts.description: Mainstream ZFC beyond the basics: large cardinals, constructibility, and Cohen's forcing / independence proofs (Smith §12.4).

concepts.name: Large Cardinals
concepts.description: Strong axioms of infinity: the hierarchy of large cardinal hypotheses reaching beyond ZFC (Smith §12.4).

concepts.name: Forcing and Independence Proofs
concepts.description: Godel's constructible universe and Cohen's forcing: proving statements like the Continuum Hypothesis independent of ZFC — with Boolean-valued models as an algebraic route in (Smith §12.4).

concepts.name: Formal Arithmetic and Computability
concepts.description: Models of Peano Arithmetic, Godelian incompleteness, recursion theory, and computational complexity (Smith §12.3).

concepts.name: Nonstandard Models of Arithmetic
concepts.description: First-order PA's uncountably many non-isomorphic models, what they reveal about what PA can and can't prove, and non-Godelian examples of incompleteness (Smith §12.3).

concepts.name: Godelian Incompleteness
concepts.description: The incompleteness theorems and the diagonalization and self-reference underlying them — including abstract routes via hereditarily finite sets (Smith §12.3).

concepts.name: Recursion Theory
concepts.description: Recursive function theory: the classical theory of effective computability, undecidability, and degrees (Smith §12.3).

concepts.name: Computational Complexity
concepts.description: Feasible computation: P, NP, and the resource-bounded refinement of computability theory (Smith §12.3).

concepts.name: The Axiom of Choice
concepts.description: The status and consequences of AC, and the wider question of which set theory to adopt: NBG, SP, NF, ZFA (Smith §12.5).
aliases: Choice and the Choice of Set Theory

concepts.name: Alternative Set Theories
concepts.description: Set theories beyond ZFC — NBG's classes, Scott–Potter's levels, Quine's NF, anti-foundation — and how to choose between them (Smith §12.5).

concepts.name: Proof Theory
concepts.description: Substructural logics, ordinal analysis, and the proof-theoretic strength of arithmetic (Smith §12.6).

concepts.name: Substructural Logics
concepts.description: Logics from varying the structural rules (weakening, contraction): relevance, linear, and other substructural systems (Smith §12.6).

concepts.name: Ordinal Analysis
concepts.description: Gentzen's consistency proof for PA via induction to epsilon-0, generalized: measuring a theory's strength by the transfinite induction its consistency proof requires (Smith §12.6).

concepts.name: Type Theory and the Lambda Calculus
concepts.description: Higher-order logic, simple type theory, the Curry-Howard correspondence, and homotopy type theory as a foundation (Smith §12.7).

concepts.name: The Lambda Calculus
concepts.description: Untyped and simply typed lambda calculus: the calculus of functions underlying type theory and functional computation (Smith §12.7).
prerequisites: Type Theory and the Lambda Calculus

concepts.name: The Curry-Howard Correspondence
concepts.description: Propositions-as-types: proofs as programs, implication as function type — the bridge between proof theory and typed lambda calculi, rooted in the intuitionistic BHK reading (Smith §12.7).

concepts.name: Homotopy Type Theory
concepts.description: Univalent foundations: type theory enriched with homotopy-theoretic ideas, advertised as a new foundation for mathematics (Smith §12.7).

tracks.goal: An advanced-reading roadmap across the subfields of mathematical logic (Peter Smith, Beginning Mathematical Logic, Ch. 12).
